routes.difficulty_description: 45-50 degree ice slopes, ~800 ft of technical climbing
watch_out: Crevasse danger in high sections: endless bottom crevasse in snowfield near top
Serac exposure on upper terrain above 11,000 ft
Two significant ice pitches (35-40 and 45-50 degrees) requiring ice tools and protection
Fixed rappel line may or may not be present at drainage gully - verify current conditions
Snow bridge hazards documented in lower snowfield
Route-finding precision critical at lower elevations to locate snow/ice ramp

routes.difficulty_description: Long alpine snow route with early season conditions
watch_out: Significant rockfall and avalanche hazard in couloir - 'move quickly' emphasized
Early sun exposure warms frozen rock and triggers rockfall - early alpine start essential
Icefall exposure descending via Kautz Glacier: pass underneath looming Kautz Ice cliff and move very quickly
Large crevasses on upper Wilson Glacier requiring lengthy end-runs in late season
Bergschrund crossing potential routefinding complications at base of couloir
Soft afternoon snow conditions create hazardous descent - alpine start critical
Long day car-to-car ascent increases exposure window
Helmets highly recommended
Scout Wilson Glacier crossing from high camp evening before

routes.grade: AI2-3 with Grade IV overall, 40-60 degree ice climbing
routes.difficulty_description: Sustained steep ice and snow with serious alpine commitment
watch_out: Objective hazards from Thumb Rock itself and rock bands above at 10,500 ft bivy area
Active avalanche discharge documented: 'Avalanche from Willis Wall' photo evidence of active snow sloughs from adjacent terrain
Vertical ice pitches at bergschrund crossing (5-8 ft sections)
70-80 degree ice pitches near rock bands around 10,500 ft - crux terrain
Sustained 40-60 degree ice climbing approaching Liberty Cap Glacier
50-60 degree steep snow/ice faces on east ridge above Black Pyramid
Seasonal bridges and icy ledges require careful crevasse navigation
Intricate Carbon Glacier crossing - complex crevasse systems throughout
Extreme wind exposure at altitude: documented 80mph summit winds
Grade IV route - serious alpine undertaking requiring solid ice climbing and glacier travel skills
sources: Mountain Project route description and photos

routes.difficulty_description: Steep snow/ice climbing via cirque and narrow couloirs
watch_out: Substantial icefall exposure from Kautz Ice Cliff when accessing headwall
Must move rapidly through lower access chutes at 10,000 ft - minimize time under icefall hazard during warm months
Headwall receives a lot of sun in summer - accelerates rockfall and ice instability
Alpine start strongly recommended to minimize heating exposure
Numerous hidden holes on Nisqually Glacier crossing - rope up for safe passage
Rock bands at 11,300 and 12,200 ft may require scrambling or protection
Route best attempted when snow covers most of face
Direct glacier approaches melt out early in summer - resort to alternate access routes
Bergschrund hazard at both initial glacier crossing and Wilson Glacier approaches in late season

routes.grade: 40-55 degree ice and snow, AI2-3 with fixed rock section
routes.difficulty_description: Steep alpine snow/ice with icefall exposure and technical rock
watch_out: Bergschrund crossing requires finding bridge just left of rock outcropping - end-run may be necessary if impassable
Icefall danger: climbing between rock buttress (right) and icefall (left) with angles 45-55 degrees
Hanging ice cliff nicknamed 'doom cliff' - serious objective hazard
One short vertical rock step with one or two fixed pins - may be missing or unreliable
Condition-dependent: may encounter almost entirely snow climb OR substantial climbing on verglassed rock and water ice
Requires ice screws, pins, and rock gear depending on conditions encountered
Sustained steep snow climbing 40-55 degrees on ~1000 ft summit day gain
High elevation remote position on northwest side
Limited bivy sites until reaching Liberty Cap Glacier
Steeper and more sustained than adjacent Liberty Ridge - demands experienced fit climbers
sources: Condition variation documentation in trip reports

routes.grade: AI2-3 with Grade IV variation, 50-60 degree steep snow and ice
routes.difficulty_description: North face ice and mixed terrain above Mowich Lake
watch_out: Steep ice sections (AI2-3 grade) requiring technical protection
Very steep snow pitches 50-60 degrees on Grade IV variation
Mixed rock and ice terrain at 12,500 ft elevation
Grade IV line features sustained steep climbing with significant exposure
'Hour glass' snow, ice and rock terrain suggests terrain traps and instability
Limited protection placement opportunities on extended snow sections: only 'couple of screws for ice steeps and 2-4 pickets'
Two route variations (Grade III cuts left, Grade IV cuts right) - complexity adds navigation hazard
No explicit avalanche advisories provided - use judgment in snow stability assessment
Must arrange descent via Emmons or Disappointment Cleaver - navigation complexity

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier travel across convex slopes to upper Coleman and Deming glaciers
watch_out: Serac avalanches do occur here with some regularity - particularly in Black Buttes area below Colfax Peak
Multiple crevasse systems requiring proper rescue equipment
Major crevasse formations documented: teams navigating large crevasses at 8,800 ft
Active icefall hazards on route - climbers must navigate around
Convex snow slopes increase crevasse hazard - crevasses are hidden
Must bring snow pickets and crevasse rescue gear for safe passage
Roman Wall section requires careful navigation around upper Coleman and Deming glaciers
Grade II designation reflects moderate technical difficulty but substantial objective hazards
sources: Mountain Project route description and trip reports

routes.difficulty_description: 800 feet of steep ice climbing on northwest face
watch_out: Upper section: 800 feet of 60-degree ice (AI4, WI3) with little reliable protection
Ice slab instability: upper section ice slabs formed giant plate (1 inch thick) covering wet snow underneath
Two bergschrund crossings requiring careful navigation
Evidence of avalanche activity along route
Steep snow sections reaching 50 degrees in lower portions
Thin ice layers prone to instability and difficult climbing
Alpine terrain on northwest face involves significant weather exposure
Limited anchor options on main climbing sections - belays below rock bands use ice screws, upper pitches use pickets punched through ice layer
Seasonal variation in snow and ice stability - early May differs from later ascents
Monitor cascadeclimbers.com for current conditions
sources: Early May trip report with ice condition details

routes.difficulty_description: Long remote glacier route on Baker's northeast flank
watch_out: Crevasses requiring navigation through crevasse fields
Headwall section requiring careful route-finding
Long, remote route on Baker's northeast flank increases commitment and rescue difficulty
Glacier gear essential - carries full objective hazard of glacier travel
sources: Mountain Project route description (limited hazard detail available)

routes.difficulty_description: Exposed chimney system with ice, snow, and rock scrambling
watch_out: Steep and exposed terrain throughout - particularly dangerous during descent
Exposed down climbing through chimney sections requiring rappel stations
Active rockfall zones in off-route gullies - warning about 'moss or lichen covered rock' indicates these trap hazards
Large crevasses on Upper Curtis Glacier requiring standard glacier travel skills
Moat hazard: 'Watch out for the moat' near initial snow crossing
Upper Curtis Glacier starts to break up late season - deteriorating conditions create hazards
Late season when Winnie's Slide becomes iced: may need ice screws and second tool
Route-finding extremely difficult - 'People frequently report difficult route finding. Pay attention and don't overthink it!'
Multiple thin paths lead to cliff overlooks - serious hazard for disoriented parties
Significant weather exposure on dramatic Upper Curtis Glacier crossing
2-3 day commitment increases exposure risk
Self-registration required at Glacier ranger station for multi-day attempts
sources: Mountain Project route description and user warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Technical alpine rock with mixed terrain
watch_out: Loose rock and sometimes crumbly black lichen on rock at times
Alternative bypass gully NOT recommended - involves climbing dirty rock prone to rockfall
Gendarme section almost always in shade - can be very cold or even icy, creating hypothermia risk and icy climbing conditions
Significant complex terrain: 'parties have accidentally taken wrong descent path' - disorientation hazard
Seasonal constraints: snow patches affect water availability at notch (8,200 ft)
Multiple approach/descent options involve glacier travel (Sherpa, Stuart glaciers) that become 'too difficult to navigate' as season progresses
Route quality varies - some sections clean, others lichen-covered and unstable
20 pitches of mostly 5th class climbing with mixed terrain
Helmet essential due to rockfall concern
Complex terrain and poor visibility increase navigation challenges especially during descent
sources: Mountain Project route description and hazard warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Mixed ice and snow couloir on ice
watch_out: Water ice pitches up to 60 degrees - hardest ice conditions
Most of route at 40-50 degree angles (unstable snow/ice)
Crux: approximately 100 feet of water ice up to 60 degrees requiring ice climbing technique
Mixed terrain transitions between ice and rock - protection quality variable
Alpine ice climbing requiring strong technical skills

routes.difficulty_description: Classic North American alpine climb with mixed terrain
watch_out: Loose rock and lichen-covered sections throughout
Complex terrain with multiple approach/descent route options
Glacier travel hazards (Sherpa, Stuart glaciers) particularly late season
Water availability limited at notch (8,200 ft) depending on snowmelt
Navigation complexity - route-finding can be challenging
Weather exposure on open alpine terrain
Significant elevation gain over long day commitment

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine ice climbing on glacier terrain
watch_out: Alpine ice terrain with technical climbing
Crevasse hazards inherent to glacier climbing
Bergschrund crossing required
sources: Mountain Project Mount Stuart area page

routes.difficulty_description: Complex alpine ice and snow couloir climbing
watch_out: NOT recommended for beginners - 'really requires great deal of soloing or run-out climbing'
Sustained steep snow and ice pitches ('the runnels') separating first and second couloirs
Pitches take ice screws depending on conditions OR pitons and rock gear - variable protection
Significant unprotected climbing sections - serious consequences for mistakes
Requires comfort on steep snow and WI3 climbing ability
Photos document 'whiteout on descent' and 'bad weather' - rapid condition deterioration likely
High alpine location (Dragontail Peak in Enchantments, >9,000 ft) - weather can turn quickly
Couloir format inherently presents rockfall danger
Sparse natural protection on mixed terrain: small nuts, 4-5 pins, cams (.5-2) and 4-5 ice screws needed
Advanced winter mountaineering abilities required
sources: Mountain Project route description and photos

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine snow couloir with variable steepness
watch_out: Constant 40-50 degree angle with slightly steeper sections half way up depending on year
Middle section can melt out early in season - creates instability and avalanche risk
Rock band near summit requires navigation through exposed stone
Objective hazard from falling rock during warmer conditions when snow melts around exposed stone
Seasonal guidance suggests 'Late May and early June typically have best conditions' - poor conditions outside this window
Endless bushwhacking through slide alder on approach - adds fatigue and cumulative exposure time hazard
Grade III alpine terrain demands winter mountaineering experience and conservative decision-making
Protection needed: pickets if uncomfortable with steep snow/easy ice, set of stoppers, webbing for raps

routes.difficulty_description: Steep ice climbing with variable conditions
watch_out: Route is condition-dependent: 'When fully in, Razorblades offers 3 fantastic pitches of ice'
May not be climbable during all conditions - early season may have poor ice formation
Steep funky climbing on first pitch - suggests unstable or unpredictable ice conditions
Crux pitch (P1): WI5+, 120 feet - steep technical climbing with high difficulty
Second pitch: short steep curtain (WI4) - bulges and difficult placements possible
Third pitch: long straight forward WI5 - endurance and protection placement challenge
Alternative option: can walk around pitch 1 on left if not solidly formed - early season workaround
Water flow hazard: large chunk of greenish ice pouring down cliffs indicates active water flow feeding ice formation
Active water affects both conditions and ice stability
Ice screws required for protection

routes.difficulty_description: Mega classic with multiple ice pitches and avalanche terrain
watch_out: Approach crosses avalanche terrain - documented D1 avalanche debris observed ('enough to kill you')
Drainage itself marked as avalanche-prone - high risk of wet slab activity
River crossing hazard: frozen river must be crossed on approach
Deceptive conditions: 'If river isn't frozen at slow points, flow is probably also deceivingly not frozen' - may be impossible or dangerous during warm periods
Tentative ice quality on initial pitches - highly variable conditions
Must search for 'most frozen line' rather than following established passages
Thick ice sections unreliable for anchors - protection placement challenges
Climbing difficulty ranges WI3-WI4+ depending on ice formation and line choice
P1: WI2+/WI3 finding most frozen line up main flow
P3-5: varying WI3-WI4+ with difficult start to crux flow
Mixed terrain with technical moves on steep snow traverses
Semi-hanging belays on upper pitches with limited protection
Weather-dependent formation: may appear frozen from road but be PG-13 slush conditions when approached
Climbers frequently find deceptive conditions despite solid appearance from distance
sources: Mountain Project route description and condition warnings

routes.difficulty_description: Classic Icicle Creek ice formation
watch_out: Icicle Creek subject to seasonal raptor closure: 1/2 mile radius of Bridge Creek Wall closed January 1 through August 15 for golden eagle nesting
Conditions dependent on winter freeze-thaw cycle - not reliable every season
High water hazard during thaw periods
Approach hazards typical of creek canyon access
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Hubba Hubba area ice climbing
watch_out: Subject to same seasonal raptor closure as other Icicle Creek routes
Seasonal ice formation unreliability
Creek canyon approach hazards
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Mixed ice and climbing on buttress
watch_out: Eightmile Buttress location subject to seasonal conditions
Mixed climbing requiring both ice tools and rock gear
Raptor closure applicable to Bridge Creek Wall area
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Creek ice area page

routes.difficulty_description: Committing alpine couloir and ridge route
watch_out: Extremely committing - very long approach with lots of up and down: difficult to retreat once committed
Total mileage 40+ miles (up Frostbite, down Cool Glacier) - multi-day commitment increases exposure
Most parties take 2-3 days - extended alpine exposure risk
Seasonal variation: 'Route may be snow in early season, expect more ice later in year'
Snow slopes up to approximately 45 degrees with steep angle exposure
Class 3 rock scrambling sections on ascent
Alpine ice face requiring 1-2 pitches of technical climbing
'Rabbit ears' notch landmark can be disorienting - navigation hazard
Careful navigation required to avoid steeper snow/ice climbing beyond rabbit ears
Must know descent route and consider pre-programming GPS coordinates
Infrastructure issues: 'best approach may vary year to year' due to occasional washout of bridges/roads
North Fork Sauk Trail may be best starting/ending point - long approach logistics
Equipment: rope, pickets, ice screws with small to medium nuts optional

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier approach to Glacier Peak summit
watch_out: Glaciated terrain requiring technical glacier travel skills
Ice axe and crampons essential for crevasse hazard mitigation and steep snow
High elevation (10,541 ft) with significant altitude exposure
Navigate corridor left of feature dividing glacier's lower section
Snow chute near summit - avalanche potential on steeper terrain
9-mile approach with significant elevation gain to base
High alpine location indicates substantial exposure to mountain weather
Extended glacier crossing creates commitment hazard
Route lacks explicit warnings about specific hazards like avalanche danger or crevasse fields
sources: Mountain Project route description (limited hazard detail)

routes.difficulty_description: Ridge and glacier route on Glacier Peak
watch_out: Alpine ice and mixed terrain exposure
Glacier travel hazards on Cool Glacier
Navigation complexity through multiple basins

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier climbing on Washington's second highest peak
watch_out: Alpine ice terrain at high elevation (12,276 ft)
Steep snow slopes requiring careful glacier travel
Multiple glacier systems on mountain create navigation complexity
Weather exposure at summit altitude
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Snow ridge approach to summit
watch_out: Moderate snow climbing at high elevation
Ridge traverse creates exposure hazard
Water availability at notch dependent on seasonal snowmelt
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Ice and steep snow on north face
watch_out: Alpine ice climbing requiring ice tools and crampons
Steep snow sections with exposure
PG13 rating indicates moderate protection and hazard concerns
sources: Mountain Project Mount Adams area page

routes.difficulty_description: Alpine mixed climbing on remote peak
watch_out: Remote mountain - long approach and difficult rescue access
Mixed ice and rock climbing requiring versatile skills
Alpine terrain with weather exposure
Mount Index described by Fred Beckey as 'one of most difficult summits to attain in the Cascades'
North Peak approach hazards
Loose rock typical of Cascade peaks
sources: Mountain Project Mount Index area page

routes.difficulty_description: Scrambling with steep rock sections
watch_out: Chossy steep section in upper portion - loose or unstable rock
Summit block has 'steep and roofy overhead' terrain - exposure on direct finish
Solid red rock in lower sections transitions to smooth high-featured slab mid-section
Most climbers do this unroped despite class 3-4 terrain - exposure is real
Route-finding tricky, going too far west toward Red Mountain Pond indicates navigation challenges
Summit section decision point: easier scrambling (west side) or harder, exposed climbing (direct finish)
Descent sections could use rappelling equipment

routes.difficulty_description: Glacier and rock approach via Cascade Pass
watch_out: Roping up on glaciers generally good idea - Sahale Glacier small and not very active
3rd and 4th class scrambling after glacier - exposed terrain
Short chimney-like feature to summit - route-finding required
Final pitch rated 5.0 (5th class rock) - falling rock potential
Grade II alpine classification indicates moderate technical difficulty
8,500 ft elevation - high alpine weather exposure
Avalanche danger at Cascade Pass during high-risk periods
Route lacks specific warnings about crevasse fields, weather exposure, or avalanche risk assessment

routes.difficulty_description: Alternative approach via glacier and rock
watch_out: PG13 rating indicates moderate hazard concerns and moderate protection
Glacier approach with exposure on final sections
4th class rock scrambling with falling rock potential

routes.difficulty_description: Legendary traverse across North Cascades peaks
watch_out: 2-3 day alpine commitment with multiple peaks crossed
Mixed terrain with rock, ice, and snow
Extensive glacier travel across multiple systems
Weather exposure on high altitude ridge traverse
Navigation complexity across multiple peaks and saddles
Spider Glacier crossings and other technical sections

routes.difficulty_description: Granite alpine rock climbing
watch_out: Snow and ice present early in season through June
Best climbing season: late June through September
Exposed alpine terrain with weather hazard
Rappel descents on multi-pitch routes
Rock quality varies - loose sections possible
Northwest Forest Pass or America The Beautiful Pass required for trailhead access
Early season climbing requires knowledge of current conditions
sources: Mountain Project Washington Pass area page

routes.difficulty_description: Direct glacier headwall ascent avoiding crevasses
watch_out: 45-degree headwall of Ingraham Glacier - steep snow/ice section
Avoids many small and some huge crevasses - route planning critical
Upper portion has low angle switchbacks - less steep but route-finding required
Summit rim attainment at 14,100 ft - altitude exposure
20-30 minute final walk from crater rim to Columbia Crest
Summer conditions bring hidden crevasse hazards and moats

routes.grade: Hourglass Gully winter variation: 4th class, 1 pitch
routes.difficulty_description: Winter alpine variation on Mount Index
watch_out: Winter route requiring ice climbing or snow climbing skills
Short ice pitch potential in winter conditions
Alpine terrain exposure in winter conditions
Remote location increases rescue difficulty
sources: Mountain Project Mount Index area page

routes.name: Hubba Hubba
routes.access: Icicle Road off US-2 near Leavenworth, approximately 6-8 miles up canyon. Popular beginner ice climb.
watch_out: Avalanche terrain: Multiple converging avalanche gullies with 30-45 degree slopes. Frequent avalanches on approach and descent routes.
Soft slab avalanche potential: observed avalanche activity between Doctor Creek and Victoria Creek at 5,500 ft on north-facing aspects
Ice quality highly variable: Thin and rotten ice common in transition zones; hollow ice in lower sections; water-spitting holes; melting pillars and detached ice during warm conditions
North-eastern aspect provides late-season ice preservation - best conditions January-February; susceptible to rapid deterioration during warm periods
Temperature-driven transitions from dry to moist snow (moist below 3,500 ft, dry above) - affects both ice quality and avalanche stability
Wind-loaded slopes above: Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm at 4,500 ft) creates hidden instability; variable bonding makes slope stability assessment critical
Rappel descent - verify anchor quality on ice; be aware of loose rock and ice debris below major flows
Route stays in condition longer than southern-facing alternatives due to orientation - check recent Mountain Project trip reports before approach
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

routes.name: Center Flow
routes.access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road. Large flow when conditions are good.
watch_out: Water ice dependent on flow - varies by year and winter precipitation patterns; flow can be large when in good shape
Exposed aspect to temperature swings causing freeze-thaw cycles
Adjacent avalanche terrain: soft slab terrain at 5,000-5,500 ft elevation
Two-pitch climb with ledge between pitches - belay stances have bolted anchors on left (rock wall on right side alternative)
Descent hazard: two-rappel descent requires careful anchor assessment
Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm deep up to 4,500 ft) creates hidden instability in surrounding terrain
Route condition highly variable - check recent Mountain Project trip reports before approach
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

routes.name: Left of Hubba Hubba
routes.access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road. Small flow to the left of main Hubba Hubba flow. Can be 1-2 pitches depending on conditions.
watch_out: Thin ice on upper pitch - WI2-3 conditions on slab section requiring precise ice tool placement
Lower angle slab ice more susceptible to freeze-thaw breakage than steep flows
Avalanche exposure: north-facing background slopes at ~5,500 ft - monitor wind slab development
Serac/icefall hazard: adjacent waterfalls above route can shed ice - time ascent to avoid peak melt periods (early afternoon)
Moist snow transition (below 3,500 ft) affects base conditions and descent safety
Belay ledge ice quality varies significantly - variable ice conditions require pre-trip assessment
Two-pitch descent requires good route-finding in winter conditions
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

routes.name: Buttress Left
routes.access: Eightmile Buttress area, Icicle Creek Road (mile marker 6.4). GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518. Pitch 1: climb left-running ramp with belay on exposed rock. Pitch 2: traverse right across buttress to WI3 ice below roof, top out onto steep snow.
watch_out: Mixed climbing (M2) requires rock tool placement on ice-covered granite - unpredictable rock ice adherence
Transitional climbing terrain between ice and rock creates anchor placement difficulty
Avalanche terrain: Eightmile Buttress faces east/north - morning ascents recommended to avoid afternoon warming
Soft slab avalanche risk from wind-loaded slopes above (Christmas crust layer creates weak substrate)
Serac hazard from icefall flows above - monitor for rockfall/ice debris
GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518 - area shows recent avalanche activity in NWAC observations
Descent: traverse climber's left, rappel with two 70m ropes to P1 webbing anchor. Alternative: bolt anchors on exposed rock (right), or possibly walk-off upper and far right
Two-pitch route - sustained commitment with limited escape options once committed
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

routes.name: Icicle Buttress
routes.access: Icicle Creek Road, mile marker 6.4, elevation 2,090 ft (637 m). GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518. Multiple ice and mixed lines on buttress: left, middle, and right sides.
watch_out: Highly variable conditions - left side (lower angle slab, often thin) vs right side (steeper WI4 ice, sustained) vs middle (occasional steeper sections)
Left side: thinner ice on slab requiring delicate footwork and ice tool precision; susceptible to freeze-thaw degradation; WI2-WI3 upper pitch
Right side: steeper ice (WI4) with sustained climbing - higher exposure to falling ice and serac hazard
Middle section: occasionally steeper ice appears creating unexpected grade surprises and protection challenges
Water ice formation depends on flow - highly season and year-dependent; poor formation in low-precipitation winters
Rock gear necessary for mixed sections - M2-M4 climbing possible on steeper sections with rock/ice transitions
Avalanche terrain: entire buttress area exposed to soft slab hazard from north-facing slopes above
Serac/icefall: upper sections have active ice flows that can shed large blocks - time climbing to avoid afternoon melt
Wind-loaded slopes above (confirmed Christmas crust layer up to 4,500 ft) create ongoing instability
Limited screw placements on thin sections - may require unconventional anchor rigging
Multiple pitch descent routes - route-finding required in winter whiteout conditions
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall 1/2-mile buffer closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
High commitment three-pitch climb with serious consequence if retreat becomes necessary

routes.name: Eightmile Buttress Ice (General)
routes.access: Eightmile area, Icicle Creek Road, approximately 8 miles up canyon from highway 2
watch_out: Multiple unnamed lines with highly variable conditions - requires current beta from local climbers
Elevation range 3,500-5,500 ft - transition zone between moist and dry snow affecting both ice quality and avalanche character
Avalanche terrain: east/north-facing slopes above buttress show documented soft slab activity
Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm deep up to 4,500 ft) creates hidden layer instability - assess slope sensitivity before committing to approach
Waterfall ice sensitive to flow variations - check current water conditions before trip
Serac/icefall hazard: multiple active ice formations above routes - descend early to avoid afternoon ice fall
Rapid temperature swings (freezing rain crust documented up to 4,500 ft) create dangerous freeze-thaw cycles
Isolated cracking and small loose avalanches reported - human-triggered soft slabs likely on steep terrain
Route-finding hazard: winter whitout conditions combined with multiple unnamed lines increase descent complexity
Access issue: road conditions variable through end of 2026; verify trailhead access before traveling

routes.name: Colchuck Lake approaches (Alpine Ice)
routes.access: Eightmile Road, 3.8 miles from fork to trailhead at 3,400 ft. Colchuck Lake hike gains 1,100 ft to glacier approaches
watch_out: Crevasse hazard: glaciated terrain on approach to Colchuck Col and Dragontail - no documented bridges but crevasses present
Hidden snow bridges: early season snow can conceal waterfall holes and crevasse entries - rope team recommended
Glacier ice can develop icy conditions by mid-season - ice tool and crampons essential
Avalanche exposure: north-facing slopes (Colchuck Col approach) susceptible to soft slab hazard
Serac/icefall: glacier calving on upper sections - monitor for falling ice blocks during ascent
Mixed terrain: Class 3-4 scrambling mixed with ice sections - requires ability to rope up quickly
Descent hazard: downclimbing on steep snow and ice with limited anchor options - fall consequence severe
Seasonal window tight: best conditions late season (March-May) when consolidated but before heavy melt
Road access: Eightmile Road closed or difficult through winter - verify current conditions
Winter route-finding: whiteout conditions eliminate landmarks - map/compass/GPS navigation essential
Cold temperature inversion: moist snow below 3,500 ft transitions to dry above - assess stability carefully

routes.name: Millennium Wall
routes.access: Icicle Creek area (exact location requires local beta). Training and development area with reliable ice.
watch_out: Running water on frozen waterfalls - active water flow affects ice quality and creates dangerous thin sections
Difficult route-finding in winter conditions - blazes and landmarks obscured by snow
Creek crossings on approach - stream crossings during high water periods can be dangerous
Thin ice requiring unconventional anchor rigging - partial penetration screws may require creative solutions
South-facing exposure: sun-driven melting limits climbing windows to morning/early afternoon
Avalanche terrain: approach slopes can slide during warming periods
Descent hazards: downclimbing potential if unable to rappel from top - verify descent options before committing

routes.name: Rainbow Falls
routes.access: Icicle Creek area (specific location requires local knowledge). Accessed via waterfall approach.
watch_out: Waterfall ice conditions highly variable - water flow changes affect ice quality hour-to-hour
Running water creates unpredictable thin sections and hollow ice - visual inspection essential
Creek crossing hazard on approach - water level affects accessibility
Water-spitting holes common at base of falls - avoid belaying directly below waterfall
Serac/icefall hazard: active waterfall means unstable ice blocks possible - timing critical
Difficult approach route-finding in winter - tricky navigation to base of flow
Limited descent options - evaluate retreat plan carefully before committing to climb
Seasonal variability: highly dependent on water flow and temperature patterns

routes.name: Plastic Fantastic
routes.access: Icicle Creek vicinity, exact location requires local beta
watch_out: Thin ice sections - requires careful footwork and ice tool precision
Running water hazard - active water flow creates unpredictable ice conditions
Avalanche approach hazard - access slopes can slide
South-facing aspect: sun-driven melting limits climbing window
Serac/icefall potential from adjacent ice formations
Route-finding difficulty in winter - requires current beta before attempting

routes.name: Right of Hubba Hubba
routes.access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road, 6-8 miles up canyon. Actually two separate climbs to the right of main Hubba Hubba flow.
watch_out: PG13 rating indicates serious hazards - run-out sections with limited protection
Always thin ice - most times cannot take screws above 10cm; severely limited protection options
Difficult screw placement requires small-diameter ice screws or creative anchor rigging
Two flows vary drastically by conditions - require mixed climbing and trad gear for route changes
Steeper than left side - more sustained climbing with higher commitment and serious consequence of falls
Fun but 'spicy' climbing - expert mixed ice and rock skills required
Rockfall hazard: steep rock sections above ice can produce falling debris after sun exposure
Approach avalanche danger: 30-45 degree slopes prone to soft slab avalanches
Icefall hazard: adjacent flows may shed ice - monitor for unstable formations
Wind-loaded terrain above: Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm at 4,500 ft) creates ongoing instability
Descent with limited anchor options - verify retreat strategy before committing
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
Active avalanche path crossing (Phantom Slide approach) — check NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast, time crossing carefully
Thin or absent ice most winters — be ready to dry-tool, condition-dependent climbing
Loose chossy rock on pitch 2 and box gully — protection can be difficult
No established rappel stations on descent — inspect any fixed anchors thoroughly, fatal anchor failure recorded (2025)
Cold northwest-facing aspect — expect winter temperatures even on clear days
Avalanche patrol explosives nearby (Alpental) — stay clear of ski area boundary during control operations
Phantom Slide approach crosses active avalanche path — check NWAC Snoqualmie Pass forecast, time crossing for safety
Loose, chossy rock on pitch 2 and through box gully — protection challenging
Thin or absent ice most winters — expect to dry-tool, condition-dependent climbing
Cold northwest-facing aspect — summit temperatures stay freezing even on clear days
Alpental ski patrol avalanche control (explosives) nearby — be aware of blast timing, stay clear of boundary
Multiple large transverse crevasses on Lower Curtis Glacier icefall — direct hazard on climbing route
Sustained ice sections up to 70° combined with low-visibility conditions make route-finding through icefall challenging
Crevasse field navigation: mid-June through August primary season, bergschrund crossing critical early season
Standard Fisher Chimneys glacier crossings on descent — crevasse hazard and route-finding complexity
Icefall hazard on Upper Curtis Glacier — check conditions before committing to retreat via East Buttress
Thin, fragile ice common — condition varies dramatically year to year
Tool placement critical — expect poor placements, consider dry-tooling rock if ice insufficient
Loose rock throughout approach and route — be aware of rockfall potential
Route-finding complex with multiple line options (Thin Column vs. East Column) at similar grade
Rappel anchors variable quality — inspect thoroughly before committing to descent
Serac collapse hazard on Park Glacier headwall — typical time-of-day risk 10am-2pm
Crevasse field extensive — mid-June through September active season
Bergschrund crossing critical early season — may require tool use and rappel anchors
Rockfall exposure below headwall — move quickly through exposed terrain
Route-finding critical on descent in whiteout — carry detailed map/GPS/compass
Altitude ~10,700 ft — altitude sickness common on repeated attempts
Serac collapse hazard on Fuhrer Finger headwall section (10am-2pm exposure, high-risk season July-Aug)
High avalanche exposure >35° terrain — spring risk March-May, summer corn cycles
Crevasse field (mid-June through August) — bergschrund crossing critical early season
Rockfall significant below the headwall — move quickly through exposure
Mixed terrain transitions (snow/ice/rock) — crampon/axe removal/redonning required multiple times
Route-finding critical on descent in whiteout — carry detailed map/GPS/compass
High altitude (14,410 ft) — altitude sickness common on repeated attempts, summit wind 40+ knots typical
Icefall zones on upper wall — significant serac collapse hazard, observe for activity before committing
Technical ice sections WI4-5 throughout — challenging tool placement, exposure
Rockfall exposure significant — especially on rock pitches between ice sections
High altitude (14,410 ft) — altitude sickness risk, summit wind 40+ knots typical
Long commitment — limited escape options once past mid-wall
Mixed terrain transitions (snow/ice/rock) — technical execution required
Descent requires rappels on variable anchors — inspect thoroughly, may need to build new anchors
Route-finding critical in whiteout — potential for navigation errors causing major delays

routes.route_name: Mount Stuart - Cathedral Peak North Ridge
watch_out: Technical mixed climbing IV+/M5 — requires ice/rock tool proficiency and safe mixed terrain technique
Loose rock and poor protection on rock pitches — requires careful route-finding and anchor assessment
Crevasse hazard on approach glacier — microspikes/short rope team recommended
Icefall hazard on East Buttress (potential retreat route) — check conditions before committing
Exposure on summit tower — one-hand-off belay standard, take care with anchors
Descent via rappels on snow/ice — anchor quality variable, inspect thoroughly
High commitment — limited escape options once past pitch 3, plan descent carefully
Icefall hazard on Lower Curtis Glacier — serac activity possible, check conditions before climbing
Crevasse field with multiple transverse crevasses — mid-June through August active season
Ice quality variable (70° sections possible) — condition-dependent climbing, ice changes daily
Route-finding complex through icefall in low visibility — careful navigation required in clouds
Descent complexity: multiple rappels on snow/ice, anchor quality variable, rope management critical
Rockfall exposure possible on approach terrain — helmets required

routes.name: Franklin Falls - Main Falls Lines
routes.height: 70 ft main, 135 ft total with tiers
watch_out: Avalanche terrain: Steep south-facing slope above belay area; climbers in direct line of potential slides
Chandelier ice: Brittle, unstable ice in top half of routes; prone to collapse in warm weather
Spray ice: River spray ice is low-density and deceptive; boot penetration leads to water immersion
Loose rock and ice: Area experiences heavy hiker traffic; rockfall and ice chunks from other parties
Communication hazard: Waterfall noise + freeway roar make belay communication impossible; requires pre-climb plan
Mixed access: Left side routes have half-dozen lines but treacherous approach on low-density spray ice
Descent: Right-side mixed routes add technical variation; route-finding critical
routes.ice_quality: Sturdy lower half, brittle upper chandelier ice; forms during sustained freeze cycles; rare for main falls to be fully in condition
routes.seasonal_detail: Left and right subsidiary lines freeze almost every year during cold spells; main central falls rarely forms completely
routes.weather_patterns: South-facing exposure makes afternoon sun hazard; needs clear winter pattern with stable cold
sources: Franklin Falls Ice Climbing - The Mountaineers trip report

routes.name: Alpental Falls
watch_out: Avalanche danger from above: Substantial terrain above fall lines can produce slides; rightmost lines provide best protection
Thin, funky ice: Patches of thin, water-covered ice interspersed with solid sections; risk of punching through to running water or bare rock
Snow cover: Thin snow layer obscures ice quality; climb assessments difficult from below
Crux finish: Final pitch grades WI3 curtain after easy WI2 climbing; abrupt difficulty change
Rope management: 60m rope doesn't reach ground; multi-pitch descent required; crevasse rescue considerations
Seasonal ephemeral: Highly moisture-sensitive; forms only during sustained freeze cycles following coastal maritime patterns
routes.ice_quality: WI2 bulk with WI3 crux final curtain; thin and variable quality; requires 5+ days sustained below-freezing
routes.approach: 0.5 mile hike from Snow Lake trailhead parking; relatively accessible for major route
sources: Climber Kyle - Alpental Falls + Phantom Tour (Feb 2024)

routes.name: Alpental Practice Wall
watch_out: Low-angle terrain: Minimal protection; focus is practice and skill building rather than serious climbing
Top-rope setup potential: Can be arranged; assumes some bolted/equipped anchors available
Seasonal: Beginner-friendly formation requirements; more reliable than major falls
routes.ice_quality: WI1-2, mostly under WI2; reliable practice-level climbing
routes.description: Designed practice area with non-committing pitches suitable for top-rope setup; steepest grade WI2

routes.name: Kiddie Cliff
routes.pitches: Single pitch per line (left WI4, right WI3)
watch_out: Substantial avalanche hazard above: Steep terrain overhead; avalanche forecast must be checked before climbing
Inadequate rappel anchor: 70m rope from tree anchor will NOT reach ground; extended rappels or complex descent required
Snow mushroom summit: Lead on left line complicated by unstable top-out; loose snow/ice at anchor
Top-rope feasible: Can be safely top-roped from tree anchors; avoids summit exposure and rappel issues
Shaded, low-volume flow: Forms more frequently than high-volume falls but still seasonal; sustained cold required
routes.ice_quality: Lower-volume, shaded aspect; WI3-4 with shale/mixed climbing character; forms more reliably than major falls
routes.approach: 20 min walk from Source Lake exit; moderate approach
routes.description: Two distinct lines: left (WI4) = crux line with difficult top-out; right (WI3) = more moderate

routes.name: Dirty Harry's Ice
watch_out: South-facing exposure: Extreme seasonal sensitivity; only forms during rare sustained deep-freeze events
Ephemeral formation: Requires extreme cold (highs in mid-20s, lows in single digits) for 5+ days; once-per-decade occurrence
Tight gully terrain: Steep approach with bushwhacking; route-finding challenges in snow
Variable ice: Mix of low-angle (30-45 deg) and vertical sections; ice quality depends on formation sequence
Limited access window: Formed only during 'Great Freeze' type events; not a reliable destination
routes.ice_quality: Rambly, variable angle; most is low-angle (30-45 deg) with vertical sections; depends on extreme sustained cold
routes.approach: 1 hr 10 min hike from turnoff, moderate bushwhacking required, near Exit 38 and Dirty Harry's Balcony
routes.description: One-pitch ephemeral route; relies on rare extreme freeze cycles; not recommended as primary destination

routes.name: Hot Tubbs (Bryant Peak)
pitch_details: P1: 20m WI4 - Vertical water ice crux right off start
P2: 45m WI3 - Ice flows with bulge, moderate snow to tree belay
P3: 75m, 50-deg snow - Snowfield with trend toward cliff notch
P4: 40m, 60-70 deg snow - Steep north-face traverse on cliffs beneath
watch_out: Crux pitch: Immediate WI4 climbing; steep rock-hard water ice may require aid or dry-tooling
Sustained snow climbing: Pitches 3-4 involve 50-70 degree slopes; cornices and rime ice common
North face exposure: Steep snow traverse with cliffs beneath; limited fall-zone recovery
Avalanche exposure: Approach and early pitches cross terrain with overhead slide risk
Weather sensitivity: Route prone to icing and wind slab formation on north face
Descent logistics: Need ropes for rappel descent; establish anchors carefully
routes.ice_quality: WI4 pillar then WI3 ice flows + snow; quality water ice in early season; rime ice later season
routes.approach: Access via Denny Creek Trail or over Bryant Col; 2-3 hr approach
sources: Climber Kyle - New Snoqualmie Pass Ice Climbs (Jan 2019)

routes.name: Melakwa Flows (Bryant Peak West Face)
watch_out: Large snowfield above: Significant avalanche terrain; must assess stability and avoid during high-hazard periods
Numerous gullies: Dozens of minor flows can create complex terrain and route confusion in whiteout
Steep ice: WI4 climbing on sustained vertical sections; good gear placement critical
Variable ice quality: Multiple flows mean different formation timing and quality; reconnaissance needed
Serac potential: Freezing/thawing cycles in spring can destabilize flow tops
Descent complexity: Multiple rappels required; anchor quality variable on unknown routes
routes.ice_quality: WI4 sustained vertical ice; densest concentration of water ice in region; variable quality between flows
routes.description: Amphitheater of flows with at least a dozen distinct routes, 150-300 ft tall; one of best concentrations of accessible ice in Washington
routes.approach: Access via Denny Creek Trail or over Bryant Col from Alpental
sources: Climber Kyle - New Snoqualmie Pass Ice Climbs

routes.name: Rap Wall - Hotline Ramp
watch_out: Variable ice quality: Despite 'ramp' name, first 15 feet dead vertical; quality varies by line choice
Tricky traversing moves: Crux involves tactical ice tool placements on thin sections
Pillar variation: Main pillar section can form WI5-WI6; route choice critical
Formation timing: Forms 'rather slowly but consistently'; survives multiple warm-rain events
Exposure: Limited fall zone but exposed terrain; proper protection essential
routes.ice_quality: WI3 ramp transitioning to vertical; thin to solid ice depending on formation; rarely reaches pillar crux
routes.approach: ~2 miles, ~1,200 ft elevation gain from Source Lake; ~1 hr approach
routes.location_detail: 500 ft above Source Lake, eastern aspect; consistent formation area

routes.name: Rap Wall - Hotline Pillar
watch_out: Extreme difficulty: One of hardest ice pitches in Washington; technical climbing for elite climbers
Overhanging start: Slightly overhanging entry with thin feet; pull-ups and lockoffs required
Equipment demands: Specialized tools, approach shoes, and advanced protection knowledge necessary
Formation dependent: Thin ice conditions; minimal protection placements; high consequences of falls
Weather sensitive: Rime ice and wind-loading common; not recommended in poor conditions
routes.ice_quality: WI5-6+ thin ice; overhanging character; elite-level climbing
routes.description: One of hardest ice pitches in Washington state; elite-only destination
sources: Climber Kyle - Hotline Ramp and Rap Wall assessment (Jan 2023)

routes.name: Rap Wall - The 36th Chamber
watch_out: Mixed climbing complexity: Combines ice and rock; gear placement on mixed terrain difficult
Chimney formation: Tight climbing with potential for pack/body jamming
R-rated protection: Limited/serious gear opportunities; high consequence climbing
Expert-only route: Requires advanced mixed climbing skills and gear placement ability
First-ascent implications: Route quality and hazard information limited by FA status
routes.ice_quality: WI5+ technical ice in mixed/chimney setting
routes.description: Serious mixed climbing route; R-rated protection; elite climbers only

routes.name: Rap Wall - Bring Da Ruckus
watch_out: Expert-only difficulty: WI6+ grade indicates elite ice climbing and aid mixed climbing
M8+ climbing: High-grade rock and dry-tool climbing; extreme technical skill required
First ascent: Route information limited; exact hazards not fully documented
Serious protection: Likely PG-13 rating reflects sketchy anchor/protection options
Not recommended for most parties: Only experienced alpine climbers in top condition
routes.ice_quality: WI6+ technical ice; elite-level difficulty
routes.description: Modern hard mixed route; first ascent by elite Cascades climbers; expert-only

routes.name: Chair Peak - Northeast Buttress
watch_out: Mixed terrain: Rock scrambling with ice/snow climbing; protection placement variable
Avalanche hazard: Alpine terrain with overhead slopes; must assess snowpack stability
Winter-specific hazard: Rime ice common on exposed faces; visibility and wind major factors
Descent: Alpine route descent requires route-finding skills in whiteout
Exposure: Serious alpine terrain; bivvy/rescue implications
routes.ice_quality: AI2-3 alpine ice mixed with rock scrambling
routes.description: Classic moderate alpine climbing; good winter mountaineering objective from Snoqualmie Pass
sources: Mountain Project - Chair Peak NE Buttress

routes.name: Drury Falls
watch_out: Avalanche terrain (D1 category debris documented)
Tentative ice quality on initial pitches
Icefall hazard - pieces size of railroad cars shed from sun exposure
River crossing required (Wenatchee River) - crampons don't combine well with inflatable boats
PG-13 slush conditions common despite appearing frozen from road
Long descent with high objective hazard
High elevation approach (~3500 ft) in avalanche drainage

routes.name: Entiat Ice Fall
watch_out: Extremely cold winters required for formation
Remote location in coldest region of WA state
Avalanche terrain in approach drainage
High altitude ice quality variable

routes.name: Zenith
watch_out: Stiff climbing, difficult to protect
Short season (less than 3 weeks)
Forms only after 2-3 weeks of sustained cold
Forms only in banner years (5-8 year cycles)
High concentration along SR 155 - roadside hazards

routes.name: H2O2
watch_out: Stiff climbing, protection difficult
Short seasonal window
Requires sustained cold period

routes.name: The Absent Minded Professor
watch_out: Long run-out pitches
Difficult protection typical of Banks ice
Short season window

routes.name: The Cable
watch_out: Very steep thin ice
Limited protection
Banner year formation only

routes.name: Tea 'n' the Sahara
watch_out: High-grade ice climbing
Short formation window

routes.name: Bombs Over Old Baghdad
watch_out: Expert-level climbing
Rare formation conditions

routes.name: Lair of the Water Dragon
watch_out: R rating (runout hazard)
High-grade climbing with limited protection
Expert only

routes.name: Terror
watch_out: Low density spray ice - looks solid but causes boot-fill hazards
Loose rock and ice common
Avalanche chute directly above falls
Heavy hiker traffic complicates safety protocols
High freeway noise prevents communication - plan before leaving ground
Main falls rarely in condition - climb side flows

routes.name: Erebus
watch_out: Mixed climbing on low-angle flow
Spray ice hazards
Avalanche terrain
Heavy hiker presence

routes.name: Northwest Passage
watch_out: Sport bolted anchor system
Avalanche chute exposure
Spray ice quality variable

routes.name: Left Flow
watch_out: Top-rope accessible side flow
Avalanche hazard from above
Spray ice instability

routes.name: Eldorado Peak Northwest Ice Couloir
watch_out: Grade varies by season: early season thin ice (WI3+), later season steep snow
Complicated approach through remote terrain
Sitting in exposed Marble Creek Cirque
60-meter pitches to knife-edge ridge
Fast parties car-to-car in day

routes.name: Colonial Peak Watusi Rodeo
routes.area: North Cascades, Colonial Peak North Face
watch_out: Mega line climbing
Easily accessible all winter from cirque basin
Only handful of ascents since 1986 first ascent
4500 ft crampons-required terrain from basin to summit
Very steep and committing

routes.name: Colonial Peak First Date
routes.area: North Cascades, Colonial Peak North Face
watch_out: First ascent 2010 - less established
North face climbing with ice and mixed terrain
High altitude alpine route

routes.name: Dragontail Peak Triple Couloirs
watch_out: 70-80 degree water ice on crux
Remote location in Enchantments area
Avalanche terrain - timing critical for stability
Spring climb preferred but can winter ascent if conditions stable
Long day 5-9 hours one-way from Colchuck Lake
Alpine Lakes Wilderness permits required
Aasgard Pass approach bottleneck

routes.name: Pyramid Peak Northeast Face
watch_out: Challenging mixed and ice climbing
Aesthetic pyramid-shaped peak visible from Diablo Dam
Steep approach terrain
North Cascades National Park restrictions
Limited established route documentation

routes.name: Davis Peak North Face Couloir
watch_out: One of steepest vertical mile drops in Washington
Extremely remote location south of Pickets Range
High precipitation = heavy wet snow and high avalanche danger
Maritime influence - wet snow conditions
Difficult access via Gorge Lake area
First ascent 1972 - not well established
Very high objective hazard

routes.name: Graybeard Peak North Face
watch_out: Only climbable 2-3 weeks after Highway 20 opens
Spindrift and icefall exposure
80-degree ice bulges
Crux: steep rock band 15-20 ft (mixed climbing, poor protection)
Poorly consolidated snow adds hazard
Cornices on ridge
45-degree pitch with water ice above crux
Limited protection on crux pitch
Recommended April-May window only

routes.name: Mount Buckner North Face
watch_out: Extremely remote Boston Basin approach
2500 ft elevation gain on rugged approach trail
Requires previous alpine experience
Efficient moderate snow/ice climbing skill required
5.6 rock scrambling comfort needed
High camp logistics on Quien Sabe Glacier
Best July-September after avalanche danger passes
Still avalanche terrain in shoulder seasons

routes.name: Mount Shuksan Sulphide Glacier
watch_out: Beginner mountaineering (no steep technical climbing)
High physical fitness required
Glacier skills essential
Summer/early fall climbing season preferred
Crevasse hazards
Weather hazards common

routes.name: Mount Shuksan Fisher Chimneys
watch_out: Steep terrain (3rd-4th class rock)
Hanging Curtis Glacier ice exposure
Steep snow and ice climbing
Significantly harder than Sulphide Glacier route
Committing terrain

routes.name: Mount Shuksan North Face
watch_out: Very steep snow and ice terrain
Bushwhack approach through dense forest
Committing mountain face
Requires excellent physical condition
Solid belaying skills needed
Moderate ice climbing ability required
Advanced crampon technique required

routes.name: Mount Shuksan Price Glacier
watch_out: Class 4 rock climbing
Alpine ice AI2-3
Technical mixed climbing
Remote and difficult route
Serious objective hazard

routes.name: Mount Baker Coleman-Deming Route
watch_out: ACTIVE VOLCANO - thermal venting and steam vents create ice caves without warning
Lahars (mud and debris flows) possible from thermal activity
Roman Mustache hanging ice and loose rock - major objective hazard, collapse without warning
Colfax Peak ice avalanches documented
40-foot wide crevasse common in recent years (August impassible)
Moat hazard: 40-foot deep in Kulshan Creek drainage (west of Hogsback Ridge at 5400 ft)
35-degree glacier sections
Whiteouts and lightning hazard
High winds common
Recent avalanche activity occurring earlier in season than historically
Move quickly through Roman Mustache section (1-2 hours)

routes.name: Big Four Ice Caves
routes.area: Big Four Mountain, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF
watch_out: DANGER: Cave-ins and slides kill climbers regularly (deaths in 1998, 2010, 2015)
Tons of ice crash from cave ceilings every year
Caves formed by melting snow and waterfalls - unstable structure
High avalanche danger from above
Trail CLOSED temporarily for safety
Do NOT climb on or enter ice caves
Popular hiker traffic increases hazard

routes.name: Salt N Peppa
watch_out: R-rated (runout) hard-to-protect traverse on loose rock
Long wet WI5 waterfall section
Poorly protected crux traverse
Expert-only climbing

routes.name: Center Flow (The Funnel)
watch_out: Water-spitting holes on upper pitches requiring careful water flow avoidance
Thin ice sections requiring half-screws and tying off placements; carry stubbers and rock gear for security
Detached or melting ice sections dependent on temperature and recent warm spells
Mixed climbing finish on pitch 2 (right-facing corner) requiring rock climbing technique
Belay anchors may be limited to slung icicles or minimal rock placements; verify before descent
North-facing aspect means ice quality extends late into season but melt-cycle sensitivity in spring
access_hazards: Bridge Creek Campground area subject to seasonal raptor (golden eagle) closure Jan 1 - Aug 15; do not approach within 1/2 mile of Bridge Creek Wall during closure
Approach via Bridge Creek Road with variable snow cover; slippery conditions underfoot
Boulder field approach through open glades with hidden boulders under light snow
routes.seasonal_window: December through March; peaks January-February; late season viability due to NE aspect
routes.ice_quality: Thick ice on central flow; prone to bonding issues after warm spells; freezing rain events create supportable crust layers but faceting risk below
sources: Mountain Project Ice Climbing in Icicle Creek

routes.name: Left of Hubba Hubba (Left Flow)
watch_out: Thin smear ice on slab; protection very difficult with rock pro and pitons required
Discontinuous ice sections requiring run-out climbing on first pitch; only short but slightly exposed
Good belay on ledge but second pitch thin and protection-challenging; ice screw placements unreliable
Lower angle approach may be slower melt-out but still susceptible to rapid degradation in warm cycles
Popular beginner route so hazards often downplayed; requires competent belay and screw placement skills
access_hazards: Same approach as Center Flow; variable snow cover and slippery conditions
Raptor closure Jan 1 - Aug 15 on Bridge Creek Wall (1/2 mile buffer)
routes.seasonal_window: December through April; excellent beginner-to-intermediate season; long viability on NE aspect
routes.ice_quality: Thin, delicate ice on slab; forms consistently but quality varies; prefers stable cold spells without freeze-thaw cycles

routes.name: Right of Hubba Hubba (Right Flow)
watch_out: Thin ice throughout; ice screws feel insecure; bring stubbers and rock gear for backup
Climbing easier than appearance from distance but ice quality demands caution
Single-pitch format limits escape options; if ice becomes unreliable mid-pitch, retreating becomes urgent
Less-protected route than center and left; fewer natural belay ledges
access_hazards: Located 50 feet right and up from main Hubba Hubba; approach same as other flows
Raptor closure Jan 1 - Aug 15 bridge creek wall
routes.seasonal_window: December through March; shorter season than left/center due to thinner ice
routes.ice_quality: Tends to be thin; forms later in season and sheds faster; sensitive to solar warming on single-pitch
sources: Mountain Project Right of Hubba Hubba

routes.name: Icicle Buttress - Left Side
routes.area: Icicle Creek - Icicle Buttress Ice Climbs
routes.height: 2,090 ft total area; multiple pitch options
watch_out: Lower-angle ice on slab can be thin and brittle; ice screw placements marginal
Slab aspect increases slipping hazard if ice degrades to water-ice film
Mixed climbing sections (M2-M3) require rock gear and climbing shoes; be prepared to switch between ice and rock
Multiple pitch options mean route-finding complexity; establish clear descent plan before committing
access_hazards: Located 6.4 miles down Icicle Road from Bridge Creek Campground
Winter road closure may require snowshoe or ski approach
Avalanche terrain; observe NWAC Icicle Creek observations for slope angle and aspect stability
routes.seasonal_window: January through February peak; shoulder season (December, early March) highly variable
routes.ice_quality: Formation depends on sustained cold and regular meltwater flow; sensitive to warm spells that thin bonding
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Buttress Ice climbs

routes.name: Icicle Buttress - Middle Section
routes.area: Icicle Creek - Icicle Buttress Ice Climbs
watch_out: Steeper ice (70-80 degrees) requiring advanced ice climbing; longer, sustained sections with fewer rests
Upper pitches can reach WI4 (continuous 80-degree ice); fatigue management critical
Mixed climbing (M2-M4) on rocks between ice; requires dual skillset and gear management
Rock gear placements may be poor quality; verify anchors before committing to top-out
Exposure increases with height; falls higher on pitch have longer run-out
access_hazards: Approach 6.4 miles down Icicle Road; winter conditions may close road
Avalanche slope assessment required; slopes facing NE/N are most stable but still require judgment
Descent route-finding in darkness or whiteout complex; plan exit before ascending
routes.seasonal_window: January and early February; upper pitches (WI4) require optimal conditions
routes.ice_quality: Steeper terrain creates thicker, more stable ice but also freeze-thaw cycling creates dinner-plating risk in afternoons
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Buttress Ice climbs

routes.name: Icicle Buttress - Right Side (Right Flow)
routes.area: Icicle Creek - Icicle Buttress Ice Climbs
watch_out: Steepest section of buttress with continuous 80-90 degree ice; WI4 sustained climbing with potential WI5 crux
Mixed climbing (M2-M4) interspersed; requires full rack of rock gear and ice screws; weight management critical
Limited rest stances; high consequence climbing on upper pitches
Ice quality can deteriorate quickly; first ascent of day preferred to assess conditions
Serac/icefall hazard if climbing in direct sun; early morning only recommended
Rope management and belay placement challenging in steep terrain
access_hazards: Same approach as left/middle; road may be closed winter
Avalanche slope assessment: right side may face more solar exposure and wind slabbing
Descent likely requires rappelling; anchors may be suboptimal; have backup anchor materials
routes.seasonal_window: January through early February only; requires optimal ice conditions
routes.ice_quality: Steeper pitch = thicker ice but also greater freeze-thaw stress; brittle ice and dinner-plating common
sources: Mountain Project Icicle Buttress Ice climbs

routes.name: Buttress Left (mixed climbing variant)
watch_out: Mixed climbing (M2) dominant; carry rock gear and ice screws in equal measure
First pitch steeper than many Icicle Creek routes; ice quality variable
Belay placement may require pitons or slings; verify before moving rope
Second pitch transitions to mixed terrain; route-finding important
access_hazards: Eightmile Buttress area; approach via Icicle Road past Hubba Hubba area
Avalanche terrain near drainage; check NWAC forecast for slope stability
routes.ice_quality: Mixed conditions; ice sections require good bonding to rock base

routes.name: A Slice of Ice
watch_out: Limited documentation available; may be minor or lesser-visited flow
Ice quality and hazards not well-established in publicly available sources
Scout conditions on-site before committing to ascent
access_hazards: Icicle Creek area general hazards apply
Raptor closure Jan 1 - Aug 15 if near Bridge Creek Wall
routes.seasonal_window: Likely December through March, similar to other Icicle flows
routes.ice_quality: Unknown; likely water-ice formation on north-facing slope
